task: I’ve got a couple of stops to line up and I’m trying to avoid a bad route choice. Can you help me think through which order makes the most sense if I want to keep driving time down and avoid getting stuck on the wrong side of town?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the cleanest way is to order the stops by **location, timing, and hard deadlines**, not just by convenience.

### Check these first
1. **Hard time windows**
   - any appointment or pickup that must happen at a set time
   - anything with a cutoff you can’t miss

2. **Geography**
   - group stops that are close together
   - avoid crossing town back and forth
   - keep the route moving in one direction if you can

3. **Traffic / access**
   - rush hour
   - one-way streets
   - parking or loading restrictions
   - areas that get jammed at certain times

4. **Stop length**
   - which stop takes the longest
   - which one is likely to have waiting time
   - whether one stop is more predictable than the others

### Simple rule for stop order
- Do the **hardest time-sensitive stop first**
- Then do the **closest logical stop**
- Then finish with the stop that is most flexible

### Quick routing test
Ask:
- **Which stop is least flexible?**
- **Which stop puts me on the wrong side of town if I do it last?**
- **Which stop adds the most drive time if I leave it for later?**
- **What happens if one stop runs long?**

### Practical way to choose
If one stop has a fixed window, that usually drives the route.  
If none of them do, start with the stop that:
- is farthest out of the way
- is hardest to reach later
- lets you move in a clean loop instead of backtracking

### Simple rule
Pick the order that:
- protects the hard deadline
- reduces backtracking
- avoids peak traffic
- leaves the most flexible stop for last

If you want, I can help you sort a real set of stops into the best order.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right framework, but it’s still pretty general. I need the actual stops to make a decent call — addresses or at least the areas, plus any time windows and which one’s the least flexible.

If you’ve got three things, I’d look at:
- exact location / side of town
- any hard appointment time
- which stop is likely to bog down or has access issues

Without that, I’m just guessing, and that’s how you end up crossing town twice for no good reason.

turns.agent_response:
Exactly. To choose the best order, I’d need at least:

- **Stop locations or areas**
- **Any hard time windows**
- **Which stop is least flexible**
- **Any access issues** like parking, loading, or gate codes
- **Which stop is most likely to run long**

Then I’d sort them by:
1. **hardest deadline**
2. **least flexible stop**
3. **best route flow**
4. **least backtracking**

If you want, send me the stops in a quick list like:
- Stop A — area — window — flexibility
- Stop B — area — window — flexibility
- Stop C — area — window — flexibility

and I’ll help you pick the cleanest order.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, here’s the list in plain form. Nothing fancy.

- Stop A — west side of Batavia, near the industrial park — no fixed window, but they like morning deliveries — pretty flexible
- Stop B — downtown Batavia — hard window 11:00 to 12:00 — least flexible
- Stop C — south of town, closer to the highway — no hard window, but loading can be slow because of dock traffic

I’m trying not to bounce back and forth across town. If I had to guess, B probably has to go first or at least before lunch, then C, then A if that keeps the loop cleaner. But if you see a better order, I’d rather do that than waste time driving in circles.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — with those stops, I’d put **B first, then C, then A**.

### Why
- **B** has the only hard window, so it drives the route.
- **C** is south near the highway, so it fits well after downtown without sending you back west too early.
- **A** is west side and flexible, so it’s the easiest one to leave for last.

### Cleanest flow
1. **B — downtown Batavia**
   - protect the 11:00–12:00 window
   - least flexible, so don’t risk it

2. **C — south of town near the highway**
   - good next stop if you’re already moving out of downtown
   - only caution is dock traffic, so don’t let it eat too much of the B window

3. **A — west side near the industrial park**
   - flexible and morning-preferred, but not hard-time locked
   - best as the last stop if you’re trying to avoid backtracking

### One caution
If **C** is known to be slow enough that it could threaten **B**, then you’d want to flip that and do **C first only if you can still make B comfortably**. But from what you gave me, **B first** is the safer call.

### Bottom line
Your guess was right:
- **B**
- **C**
- **A**

That’s the route I’d use to keep the loop clean and avoid crossing town twice.

If you want, I can also help you map the route in a **before-lunch / after-lunch** sequence.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that tracks. B first is the safe play because of the window, then C, then A. Cleanest loop, least chance of getting pinned by time or bouncing back west too early.

Only thing I’d keep an eye on is C if dock traffic starts dragging. If it’s looking ugly, you don’t let that eat into B. But with what you gave me, B-C-A is the right order.
